narration: Every finance guru tells you to spend 30% of your income on rent. But that rule is from 1969 — designed for people earning $8,400 a year. It was never meant for you.
visual: Dark background, large '30%' text springs in, person silhouette holding rent bill showing $2,057/month, year '1969' fades in below

narration: HUD created the 30% threshold to decide who qualified for housing assistance. Median rent was $200 a month back then. Your landlord didn't get that memo.
visual: Light background, house SVG with $200/month price tag, government HUD policy document stamp, year 1969 prominently displayed

narration: To actually follow the rule today, you'd need to earn $82,000 a year. The median US income is $56,000. That's a $26,000 gap — the rule is impossible for most renters.
visual: Dark background, two comparison rows: 'YOU EARN $56K' in white vs 'RULE NEEDS $82K' in amber, '$26K SHORT' text reveals in red

narration: 52% of American renters are already cost-burdened. But here's the thing — spending 35% on rent while saving 20% beats spending 28% on rent and saving nothing.
visual: Light background, '52%' stat springs in, two comparison boxes: 28% rent + 0% saved (red) vs 35% rent + 20% saved (green), SAVINGS WINS label

narration: The metric that actually matters is your savings rate, not your rent ratio. If you're saving 15 to 20% every month, your rent percentage is basically just noise.
visual: Dark background, piggy bank SVG growing with coins, savings rate progress bar filling to 20%, 'RENT% = NOISE / SAVINGS RATE = THE SIGNAL' text

narration: Next time someone quotes the 30% rule, ask them what year they think it is. Track your savings rate instead — and follow for more finance myths that need to die.
visual: Light background, phone SVG with savings calculator showing 20%, three rules appear: ignore 30% rule, track savings rate, aim for 15-20%
